--- SOURCE 1: Public-Private Partnerships in the veterinary domain ---
URL: https://www.woah.org/en/what-we-offer/improving-veterinary-services/pvs-pathway/public-private-partnerships-in-the-veterinary-domain/

SUMMARY:
Public-Private Partnerships strengthen Veterinary Services including implementation of vaccination campaigns in the animal health sector.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://www.woah.org/app/uploads/2025/01/woah-primary-logo-english-orange-black.png)

Who we are

We are the global authority on animal health. Founded in 1924 as the Office International des Epizooties (OIE), in May 2003 we adopted the common name World Organisation for Animal Health. An intergovernmental organisation, we focus on transparently disseminating information on animal diseases, improving animal health globally and thus build a safer, healthier and more sustainable world

What we do

We work to improve animal health and welfare across the globe. By collecting, analysing and disseminating veterinary scientific information, we encourage international solidarity in the control of animal health risks. Furthermore, we work across borders to foster a One Health approach, recognising that the health of animals, humans and the environment are interdependent.

What we offer

We oversee various programmes, both cross-sectoral ones and those focused on specific needs, to improve the capacities of Veterinary Services and Aquatic Animal Health Services. These programmes are designed to empower and guide countries in the development and implementation of sustainable national plans which go well beyond purely veterinary matters.

Media

Find information related to the animal disease situation in the world, as well to the work of the Organisation.

Use the search engine below to find a specific content.

# Public-Private Partnerships in the veterinary domain

To strengthen the effectiveness of Veterinary Services in the long term, collaborations between the public and private sectors are invaluable. Public-Private Partnerships enable the pooling of resources and create synergies across sectors to help improve access to services that would otherwise be unattainable by either sector working in isolation.

![Public-Private Partnerships in vet domain](https://www.woah.org/app/uploads/cache/2022/08/gettyimages-1318878086/4116574095.jpg)

## **Partnering for long-term positive impacts on animal health**

WOAH defines Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs) as a joint approach in **which the public and private sectors agree on responsibilities and share resources and risks to achieve common objectives**. The establishment of PPPs contributes to a more efficient and effective use of both public and private sector resources and enables each sector to focus on activities most central to their respective responsibilities and capabilities.

When established in a sustainable manner, PPPs can contribute to strengthening the capacity of Veterinary Services in many ways, **from policy development to the [implementation of large-scale vaccination campaigns](https://www.woah.org/en/article/involving-the-private-sector-to-upscale-livestock-vaccination/)**. They can also provide a way to balance the development and geographical presence and influence of each sector across a given country.

The relationships and capabilities that are developed through PPPs may also be utilised in [emergency management](https://www.woah.org/en/what-we-offer/emergency-preparedness/), for instance, if there is a need to respond to a disease outbreak that exceeds the capacity of the public sector.

## **Building innovative collaborations to address specific needs**

There are multiple ways to establish effective PPPs. These collaborations can take **several forms according to the type of private partners involved, the funding source, and the governance mechanisms**. They can be classified under three main typologies; however, each new PPP can comprise elements from several typologies.

### Transactional

Government procurement of specific animal health or sanitary services from private veterinary service providers.

### Collaborative

Joint commitment between the public sector and end-beneficiaries to deliver mutually agreed policies or outcomes.

### Transformative

Establishment of sustainable capabilities to deliver otherwise unattainable major programmes.

## **Supporting the establishment of effective PPPs at country level**

Despite their proven benefits in the veterinary field, PPPs often remain under-utilised. WOAH recognises the added value that this approach can have for the public sector in fulfilling its missions more efficiently and also for the private sector, by creating the conditions for better, more sustainable business.

As part of its mission to help strengthen Veterinary Services, WOAH is committed to support countries in implementing PPPs. **To help its Members explore, plan, implement and monitor such collaborations, WOAH has developed dedicated guidelines, online courses and an open-access database on PPPs.**

![](https://www.woah.org/app/uploads/cache/2022/05/18-brochure/896401174.png)
![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)

Brochure

### [Public-Private Partnerships in the veterinary domain: Learn more on how to develop impactful and sustainable PPPs in your country](https://www.woah.org/en/document/ppp-leaflet4p-en-bdpp/ "Public-Private Partnerships in the veterinary domain: Learn more on how to develop impactful and sustainable PPPs in your country")

### **Our Guidelines for PPPs in the veterinary domain**

In order to support countries in the implementation of PPPs, WOAH has produced guidelines for both the public and private sectors, based on successful field experiences. These are gathered in a Handbook.

### **Our e-learning courses**

Several courses are available online to provide a better understanding of what can be obtained from impactful and sustainable public-private partnerships in the veterinary domain.

###### **PPPs in the Veterinary Domain: An Introductory Course**

[Take the course](https://training.woah.org/course/view.php?id=200)

###### **PPPs: Opportunities for Progressive Control of Transboundary Animal Diseases**

[Take the course](https://training.woah.org/course/view.php?id=206)

###### Animal Health Surveillance and Partnerships

[Take the course](https://training.woah.org/course/view.php?id=504)

### ****Our PPP Database****

To improve further understanding of the variety of PPP options in the veterinary domain, together with the potential benefits and challenges of such partnerships,our collaborative PPP Database presents examples which have been kindly shared with WOAH by those who developed these partnerships. The aim is to provide information and opportunities to cross-pollinate successful experiences around the world.

**PPP Discussion during WOAH General Session**

During the 91st General Session of the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH), 92 participants from public and private stakeholders from Asia, Africa, the Middle East, the Americas, Europe, and Australia discussed this topic in a dedicated side event.

![](https://www.woah.org/app/uploads/2025/05/20240530-131608-1024x576.jpg)
![](https://www.woah.org/app/uploads/2025/05/20240530-133323-1024x576.jpg)
![](https://www.woah.org/app/uploads/2025/05/panel-discussion-1024x545.jpg)

**Regional PPP Workshops**

A total of 330 participants from 55 countries attended regional workshops held in various locations, including Nepal, Thailand, Ethiopia, Tunisia, Paraguay, and Bahrain. These workshops aimed to strengthen veterinary services through partnership and capacity building, providing an invaluable platform for knowledge sharing, skill enhancement, and cross-cultural collaboration among professionals from diverse backgrounds.

![200 participants representing the public and private sectors of 37 countries were trained to establish PPP, during regional workshops in Africa and Asia. ](https://www.woah.org/app/uploads/2023/05/pvs-public-private-partnerships.jpg)

**PVS- PPP Targeted Support to Members**

As a recent addition to the PVS Targeted Support portfolio in 2023, the PPP Targeted Support program has been expanded to [Sri Lanka](https://rr-asia.woah.org/en/events/public-private-partnership-and-strengthening-national-veterinary-services-in-sri-lanka/), [Ethiopia](https://rr-africa.woah.org/en/trainings/ethiopia-moves-to-enhance-amr-national-action-plan-implementation-through-public-private-partnerships-ppps/), [Tanzania](https://rr-africa.woah.org/en/news/public-private-partnerships-paving-the-way-for-sustainable-veterinary-services-in-tanzania/#:~:text=From%2018%20June%20to%2018%20July%202024%2C%20WOAH,establish%20effective%20public-private%20partnerships%20%28PPPs%29%20to%20combat%20PPR.), and [India](https://rr-asia.woah.org/en/events/public-private-partnership-ppp-targeted-support-in-india-2/).

## **How can a country benefit from PPP as part of the PVS Pathway?**

#### **WOAH Members already involved in the PVS Pathway**

*(i.e.: any Member that has completed a PVS Evaluation, Follow-up or Gap analysis mission in the five last years)*

National Delegates may send a request for PPP targeted support based on the recommendations on specific competencies/areas included the PVS mission report. WOAH can then propose a 2-3 days face-to-face national PPP workshop in the country.

#### **WOAH Members not recently involved in the PVS Pathway**

*(i.e.: any Member that has not completed a PVS Evaluation, Follow-up or Gap analysis mission in the five last years)*

National Delegates may send a request for PPP targeted support in the areas they are interested in. WOAH can then propose for a PVS Evaluation or Follow-up or Gap analysis mission. Once the final report is available, a 2-3 days face-to-face national PPP workshop can be implemented as relevant.

## More information

### An evaluation tool to strengthen the collaborative process of the public-private partnership in the veterinary domain

[Presentation by Sam Thevasagayam of the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation at the 85th OIE General Session in May 2017](https://www.woah.org/en/public-private-partnerships-as-a-means-to-support-the-capacities-of-national-veterinary-services/)

*Our activities on PPP are conducted in collaboration with Cirad and EuFMD, with the generous financial support from the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation.*

## Newsletter

The monthly newsletter that relays news from the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH), as well as from its network and regions. News includes its Members’ self-declarations, articles, upcoming events, publications, communication tools and more. Subscribe to the monthly newsletter and stay informed on the organisation’s latest activities at both global and regional levels.

# Partners for Healthy Pets

![Partners for Healthy Pets](/sites/default/files/2023-05/php-member-icon-200x223.png)

One facet of the AVMA's work to advocate for both our member veterinarians and the welfare of animals involves serving as an active partner in the nonprofit Partners for Healthy Pets initiative. Led by AVMA and the American Animal Hospital Association alongside the American Veterinary Medical Foundation, this alliance brings together dozens of veterinary associations and animal health companies to help ensure that pets receive the preventive health care they deserve through regular visits to a veterinarian.

The goals of Partners for Healthy Pets include the following:

### What does Partners for Healthy Pets do for veterinarians?

Partners for Healthy Pets has developed [tools that are available free of charge on its website](https://www.partnersforhealthypets.org/) to AVMA and AAHA members. The tools help veterinarians run more successful practices, focusing on several key areas, such as client communication; using internet marketing and social media to market a practice; creating and implementing preventive health care plans for clients; and creating a feline-friendly practice.

### Why AVMA supports Partners for Healthy Pets

Members expect the AVMA to serve as the voice of and advocate for the veterinary medical profession and the welfare of animals. With its focus on increasing the health of pets and promoting the importance of preventive care and veterinarians, Partners for Healthy Pets is one key resource to serve the needs of AVMA's member veterinarians as we strive to practice the highest quality of preventive medicine and run successful practices.

### Encouraging preventive care: Partners for Healthy Pets

Partners for Healthy Pets provides a wide range of free practice tools that help you get more pets in for regular preventive care:

AVMA is a lead partner in the Partners for Healthy Pets initiative.

### About AAVMC

# Partners

The AAVMC achieves its mission by working with a diverse group of partners, including, but not limited to, those mentioned below:

#### **Other Veterinary Organizations**

AAVMC works closely with the [American Veterinary Medical Association](https://www.avma.org/) (AVMA), and other veterinary medical organizations, including the [American Society of Veterinary Medical Association Executives](https://www.vmaexecs.org/), [Student American Veterinary Medical Association,](https://www.avma.org/savma/chapprog.asp)the [U.S. Animal Health Association](https://www.usaha.org/), the [Morris Animal Foundation,](https://www.morrisanimalfoundation.org/) and organizations representing specialty fields of veterinary medical practice.

#### **Animal Health Industry**

AAVMC works with several corporate partners in the private sector, including but not limited to the Animal Agriculture Coalition, which represents the major animal and animal-related commodity organizations in America, and the [Animal Health Institute,](https://www.ahi.org/)which represents companies with an interest in animal health.

#### **Health Professions**

AAVMC works with all of the allied health professions to support funding for training programs, especially those targeted to increasing the racial diversity of graduates. AAVMC is a member of the [Health Professions and Nursing Education Coalition](https://www.aamc.org/advocacy/hpnec/) and the Federation of Associations of Schools of the Health Professions (FASHP), which includes the [Association of Schools of Public Health](https://www.asph.org/), the [American Association of Colleges of Nursing](https://www.aacn.nche.edu/), the [American Dental Education Association,](https://www.adea.org/Pages/default.aspx) the [Association of American Medical Colleges](https://www.aamc.org/) and others. AAVMC was an important partner in establishing the [One Health Commission](https://www.onehealthcommission.org/), and also works closely with several agencies and departments of the federal government focused on human, animal, and environmental health (listed below).

#### **Animal Welfare**

AAVMC works with national, public and private sector animal care and welfare organizations to promote animal care and welfare. They include but are not limited to the Animal Welfare Division of the AVMA, the [Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service (APHIS)](https://www.aphis.usda.gov/animal_health/)Division of the USDA, the [Office of Laboratory Animal Welfare in the Office of Extramural Research (National Institutes of Health)](https://grants.nih.gov/grants/olaw/olaw.htm), the [American Humane Association](https://www.americanhumane.org/) and the [American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals.](https://www.aspca.org/)

#### **Education and Research**

AAVMC partners with several organizations and institutions devoted to promoting and strengthening education and research. These include, but are not limited to, the National Institutes of Health, the Association of Public and Land-Grant Universities (APLU), the [Association for Assessment and Accreditation of Laboratory Animal Care International](https://www.aaalac.org/), [National Association for Biomedical Research,](https://www.nabr.org/) the [National Coalition for Food and Agricultural Research](https://www.ncfar.org/), the [Howard Hughes Medical Institute](https://www.hhmi.org/), [Research!America](https://www.researchamerica.org/) and Boehringer Ingelheim, through the [Boehringer Ingelheim Veterinary Scholars program](https://www.merialscholars.com/).

#### Government Partners (Federal Agencies)

[U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA)](https://www.usda.gov/)

[Department of Defense (DoD)](https://www.defense.gov/)

[Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)](https://www.hhs.gov/)

[Department of Homeland Security (DHS)](https://www.dhs.gov/)

# CVM Puts One Health into Practice: Protecting the Health of People and Animals in our Shared Environment

![Cattle, a tree, and a couple with a dog on a paddleboard](/files/styles/landscape_16_9_800_x_450/public/OneHealth.gif?itok=KJzqJKUs "CVM One Health")

The central idea of One Health is that people, animals, and our shared environment are closely interconnected. A One Health approach to solving a public health problem considers all three perspectives to achieve the best possible outcome.

FDA’s Center for Veterinary Medicine (CVM) has long recognized the importance of One Health in its mission to protect human and animal health. Every day, CVM’s mission reaches across scientific disciplines and combines the knowledge of experts in multiple fields. Veterinarians, chemists, biologists, statisticians, economists, environmental scientists, and others work together to use science, technology, and innovation to improve the well-being of people, animals, and the environment and to support the human-animal bond.

## How CVM Uses a One Health Approach

## [We help integrate human and animal health to support a pipeline of innovative, safe, and effective products.](#689aa31167c4f)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Establishing public-private partnerships enhances veterinary health systems, pooling resources for effective disease management and promoting collaborative vaccination campaigns, ultimately improving animal health outcomes.
Prioritizing education and resource allocation for veterinarians strengthens public health initiatives, equipping professionals with vital skills to address emerging health challenges effectively.

Implementing a proactive approach to emerging zoonotic diseases ensures veterinary public health systems are resilient, integrating One Health strategies to protect human, animal, and environmental health.
